story:
The forest rose so high it seemed to touch the clouds. Damp, dark, and misty, it felt both beautiful and forbidding. Deer, elk, squirrels, and chipmunks flickered through the undergrowth, and the boys saw scat that hinted at bears and maybe mountain lions. Justin, Jake, and Elan kept to the narrow animal paths, soft with pine needles and leaves, ferns standing as tall as their shoulders. They had walked for weeks, living on what they carried and what the forest offered. Fish in cold streams were easy to spear with their pointed sticks. Days were shorter now, nights cooler, and rain often arrived as a heavy mist that soaked them anyway.

“How much farther to home?” Jake asked.

“If we’re going the right direction, we’ll get there,” Justin said.

“If the ocean is left and the mountains are right, the sun rises over there, so those are the eastern mountains,” Elan added. “We’re headed north.”

“That’s fine,” Jake said, “but how far?”

“Be patient,” Elan replied.

“Patience saves energy,” Justin said. “Breathe, look around, and enjoy this strange forest. You may never be here again.”

Jake tried. The forest was stern, but it was also grand.

By late morning the sky dimmed and the fine mist turned to steady rain. They wrapped themselves in canvas blankets and trudged on. Fern fronds bowed under dripping water; birds vanished from the sky; moss glowed on the north sides of trunks. The patter was soothing, but their clothes clung and the trails darkened.

“Do you see that?” Justin pointed. A giant redwood stood ahead, and at its base, impossibly, a smooth door. They hurried to it and knocked. A round disc slid aside over a peephole.

“I’m Justin, with Jake and Elan,” Justin called. “We’re wet. May we come in?”

“Welcome,” a voice said, warm with relief and curiosity.

Inside, the entry was dim and spacious, carved within the living tree. They hung gear on hooks, took off their shoes to protect the shining wood floor, and climbed to a brighter second level lit by candles and windows. A sitting area opened beside a small kitchen. A spiral stair curled upward toward what seemed to be a bedroom.

Their host, long-limbed and weathered, in a gray shirt, jeans, and mid-calf boots, sat on a stool. “My name is Happiness. Call me Hap. What brings you here?”

“We’re seeking the Valley of the Black Dog,” Jake said. “Do you know the way?”

Hap lifted his eyes as if seeing a map. “In the mountains there’s a long, narrow lake like an inland sea. At its northern end, a stream runs to a valley. I believe that’s the place. I’ve never gone that far, but you’re on the right course.”

Jake brightened. Elan asked how Hap came to live here.

“I found this tree long ago, already partly hollow. I carved carefully so it would stay alive. We depend on each other,” Hap said. “My village is two days away. I visit often. People are happy to see me, and I’m happy to see them.” His voice stayed soft. “My parents named me Happiness. It fits. Things break, I make mistakes, sometimes I’m hungry or lonely—but I don’t stay unhappy.”

“How?” Jake asked.

“My happiness comes from within,” Hap said. “If it’s misplaced, I find it by naming my feelings, deciding what might help, and making a plan. Action helps. Gratitude helps most—remembering what I have and who I am. And being with others. Those are my keys.”

When the rain stopped, light filtered in. The boys ate lunch with Hap, thanked him for shelter and wisdom, and set out again, angling northeast toward the mountains to find the inland sea and the stream to the Valley of the Black Dog. Patches of blue showed through the lattice of branches. Pine needles glittered. Walking behind Justin and Elan, Jake breathed deep, grateful for the forest and for his companions. With a direction, a plan, and patience, he whispered, mostly to himself, “Ain’t no stopping us now.”
